career.organization: Harris & Brown; Harris Ames & Co.; others
career.notes: Grocery → hardware → first major steel importer in New London. Partners: brother-in-law George W. Brown (1844-48), Ames (1853-57), Williams (1857-65). Agent for Davis Pain Killer. Investments in coal mines, railroads, steamboats.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow ABCFM Japan patron; she built Kobe College

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of New London 1856-1862; first steel importer
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Newton Harris rose from a Salem, Connecticut farm to become New London's mayor (1856-1862) and Connecticut state senator, while building the city's first major steel import business through a series of hardware and grocery partnerships. He also invested in coal mines, railroads, and steamboats, and his house at 130 Broad Street — designed by architect Leopold Eidlitz — is on the National Register of Historic Places.

narrative_blocks.header: Gave $100,000 to Doshisha University in Japan, 1885
narrative_blocks.body: In 1885, Harris gave $100,000 to establish the Harris School of Science at Doshisha University in Kyoto — a Christian institution founded by Joseph Hardy Neesima and supported by the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. He also funded New London's first hospital (1893), supported the YMCA and Williams School, and endowed a charitable trust at his death in 1896.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband outlived her by twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Anne Strong married Jonathan Newton Harris — New London mayor, civic philanthropist, and one of Connecticut's leading merchants — on July 19, 1869, as his second wife. She died December 19, 1916 in New London at eighty-four; Harris had preceded her by twenty years, dying in 1896.

burialCemetery.name: Cedar Grove Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: New London, New London County CT. FGA #103253. Contains H02695 Martha Anne Strong Harris and I01220 Jonathan Newton Harris.

INST041.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.
